1.synthesis:
Josiah's covenant renewal ceremony and the subsequent iconoclastic reform represent the apex of Davidic covenant faithfulness in the history of the divided monarchy. The king reads the Book of the Covenant publicly, leads the entire assembly to pledge obedience (vv. 1–3), then systematically destroys every form of syncretistic worship that Manasseh had introduced — and much that had accrued over centuries. The scope is comprehensive: Baal altars, Asherah poles, astral worship apparatus, child sacrifice sites in the Hinnom Valley, the horses of the sun, and the high places from Geba to Beersheba. Josiah even fulfills the prophecy spoken against Jeroboam's altar at Bethel three centuries earlier (vv. 15–18; cf. 1 Kgs. 13:2).

Calvin remarks that Josiah's reform was distinguished from earlier reforms (Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah) by its thoroughness and its public, covenantal framing: this was not merely administrative cleanup but corporate rededication. Matthew Henry observes the pattern — word received, heart broken, people assembled, covenant renewed, idols destroyed — as the irreducible sequence of genuine revival. Adam Clarke notes the fulfillment of predictive prophecy at Bethel as evidence that the man of God's word in 1 Kings 13 was authentic: three hundred years is a long time for a word to wait for fulfillment, but it arrived precisely.
1.historical_context: Josiah's reform extended into former Israelite territory (vv. 15–20) — Bethel, the towns of Samaria — in what was a politically bold assertion of Davidic jurisdiction over the north during Assyria's collapse (ca. 630–621 BC). The Topheth in the Hinnom Valley, where child sacrifice to Molech had been practiced, was "defiled" by making it a rubbish dump — preventing future cultic use by ritual impurity. The "horses of the sun" (v. 11) likely refer to a solar cult procession, attested in Mesopotamian religion. Josiah's actions constitute some of the most historically verifiable cult reform activity in the archaeological record of the period.
1.literary_structure: The reform narrative in vv. 1–20 is structured as a concentric expansion: from Jerusalem's Temple (vv. 4–7) outward through Judah's towns (vv. 8–14) and then into the former northern kingdom (vv. 15–20). The fulfillment note at Bethel (vv. 16–18) interrupts the sequence with a retrospective — a deliberate echo of 1 Kgs. 13:1–3 that anchors Josiah's reform in a prophetic tradition spanning centuries. The public covenant reading that opens the unit (vv. 1–3) gives legal and covenantal grounding to everything that follows.
1.theological_application:
Gill emphasizes that covenant renewal must precede and sustain structural reform: the iconoclasm without the covenant ceremony would be mere cultural revolution; the ceremony without the iconoclasm would be empty ritual. Both are necessary, and the order matters — the Word read and received generates the will for costly action. Matthew Henry applies this to the church: reformation of outward practice that has not been preceded by genuine encounter with Scripture tends to drift back. The community that hears God's word together, trembles together, and pledges together is equipped for the sustained obedience that reform requires.

Adam Clarke draws a pointed application from Josiah's extension of reform into the north: the claims of covenant faithfulness do not stop at convenient boundaries. Where we have influence, we have responsibility. The Christian who would restrict holiness to the "inner life" while leaving public and communal dimensions of life unreformed has not yet read 2 Kings 23.
1.christology: The prophecy-fulfillment at Bethel (v. 16) — named generations before the event — establishes the hermeneutical principle that undergirds the entire New Testament's reading of the Old: prophetic words find their targets across centuries. The man of God who cried against Jeroboam's altar was speaking beyond his own horizon; so were Isaiah, Jeremiah, and the Psalmists when they described the Suffering Servant, the new covenant, and the Son of Man. Josiah's reform points forward as well: even the most thorough human reformation of worship cannot finally cleanse the human heart. The greater reform comes when Christ, "by a single offering," perfects "for all time those who are being sanctified" (Heb. 10:14), accomplishing what Josiah's righteous zeal could only approximate.

21.synthesis:
The celebration of Passover described in vv. 21–23 is evaluated with a superlative the narrator reserves for no other event: "No such Passover had been kept since the days of the judges who judged Israel" (v. 22). The Chronicler's parallel (2 Chr. 35:1–19) expands the detail considerably, emphasizing the priestly organization and the scale of sacrifice. This Passover was both retrospective — commemorating the exodus — and reformative: it restored a covenant feast that the nation had apparently allowed to lapse in communal observance across centuries of apostasy.

The overall assessment of Josiah in v. 25 is the highest individual royal commendation in Kings: "Before him there was no king like him, who turned to the LORD with all his heart and with all his soul and with all his might." This echoes Deuteronomy's Shema (Deut. 6:4–5) almost verbatim — Josiah embodies the very law he found. Yet v. 26 immediately qualifies: even this superlative righteousness could not turn back the wrath kindled by Manasseh. Calvin soberly notes that Josiah's faithfulness delays but does not avert the inevitable; one righteous king cannot redeem the accumulated covenant debt of a nation.
21.historical_context: The Passover of Josiah's eighteenth year (ca. 621 BC) was a national assembly in Jerusalem, replacing the earlier tradition of household and local observance that had apparently been permitted to lapse — or had been observed only privately and without the full national covenant character. The Chronicler's account indicates priestly families were assigned specific roles and that the Levites took an active teaching role in the proceedings. This Passover served the political function of reinforcing Jerusalem's centrality as the sole legitimate worship site — directly undercutting the decentralized local shrine system Josiah had just demolished.
21.literary_structure: The Passover notice (vv. 21–23) and the comprehensive reform summary (vv. 24–25) together form the climax of Josiah's narrative before the pivot to tragedy in vv. 26–30. The superlative assessment in v. 25 is structurally positioned at the apex of the entire DH, surpassing all other kings — only to be immediately undercut by v. 26's "nevertheless." The literary effect is devastating: even the best the Davidic line produced was insufficient to overturn the accumulated weight of national apostasy.
21.theological_application: Matthew Henry's application of Josiah's Passover centers on covenant memory: a people that forgets its founding redemption loses its moral and theological bearings. The Passover was not merely a historical commemoration but a covenantal re-enactment — Israel retelling and re-inhabiting the story of its liberation. Reformed churches and believing households that allow the central covenant commemorations to lapse (baptism, the Lord's Supper) without thoughtful, engaged practice risk the same drift into functional forgetting. Gill draws from Josiah's comprehensive assessment the principle that genuine love for God must express itself through all three of Deuteronomy's dimensions — heart, soul, might — with none suppressed or substituted.
21.christology: The Passover that Josiah restored finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ, who celebrated a Passover with his disciples the night before his death and transformed it into the new covenant meal: "This cup is the new covenant in my blood" (1 Cor. 11:25). Where Josiah's Passover commemorated the blood of Egypt's Passover lamb, the Lord's Supper commemorates the blood of "Christ our Passover" (1 Cor. 5:7). The "never again a Passover like this" language of v. 22 is relativized by Calvary: Josiah's unprecedented feast was itself surpassed by the feast Christ instituted in the upper room, which remembers a Passover that no blood of animals could have accomplished.

29.synthesis:
Josiah's death at Megiddo — killed by Pharaoh Neco while marching to oppose the Egyptian advance toward Assyria — is narrated with jarring brevity after chapters of detailed reform. The reforming king dies not in battle against idolaters but in an apparently unnecessary intervention in a geo-political conflict his people did not need to join. The Chronicler adds that Neco warned Josiah he was acting "against God" and that Josiah ignored prophetic counsel (2 Chr. 35:21–22) — a note that transforms his death from tragedy into a cautionary tale about overstepping divine mandate.

Jehoahaz's three-month reign and deportation to Egypt (vv. 30–34) signals the beginning of the end: Judah is now a vassal of Egypt, its kings appointed and deposed by foreign power. Jehoiakim's installation by Pharaoh Neco and the tribute he levied on the land mark the definitive shift from sovereignty to subjection. Matthew Henry observes that Judah mourned Josiah as they had mourned no other king (2 Chr. 35:24–25) — a mourning that Zechariah connects typologically to the mourning for the pierced one (Zech. 12:11).
29.historical_context: In 609 BC, Pharaoh Neco II marched to Carchemish to support the remnant Assyrian army against Babylon. Josiah intercepted him at Megiddo — possibly to prevent a resurgent Egyptian-Assyrian coalition from dominating Canaan, or out of Babylonian alliance obligations. He was killed in the encounter. Jehoahaz (also called Shallum, Jer. 22:11) was the people's choice; Neco deposed him after three months and installed Jehoiakim (Eliakim, his brother) — the first instance of a foreign power directly controlling Judah's succession. The tribute of 100 talents of silver and one talent of gold (v. 33) represents a crippling financial burden on a kingdom already weakened by reform-era expenditures.
29.literary_structure: The unit is a hinge — closing the Josiah narrative and opening the rapid succession of kings that leads to exile. The contrast between Josiah's extended account (ch. 22–23:28) and the truncated treatment of Jehoahaz and Jehoiakim mirrors the contrast between Josiah's comprehensive righteousness and his successors' rapid apostasy. The Chronicler's Megiddo detail — Neco's divine warning and Josiah's failure to heed it — is absent from Kings, which presents Josiah's death as an unexplained reversal, heightening the theological tension: how does Judah's best king die young while her worst lived longest?
29.theological_application:
Gill's comment on Josiah's death addresses theodicy directly: even the righteous do not escape the consequences of a nation's covenant failures, and even the righteous can make fatal misjudgments. Josiah's death is not presented as punishment for personal sin but as the cost of operating within a broken covenantal world — a world in which the exile was "determined" (23:26–27) regardless of individual faithfulness. Adam Clarke applies the lesson practically: the most faithful leader must still seek divine guidance before entering a conflict. The assumption that previous faithfulness guarantees present guidance is dangerous presumption.

Matthew Henry draws the mourning for Josiah as a pattern for communal grief over godly leaders lost prematurely — "great is the loss when such shepherds are removed from their flocks." The church that loses a faithful pastor or reformer should mourn genuinely rather than assume the work will continue automatically.
29.christology: Josiah's death at Megiddo cast a long shadow in prophetic imagination — Zechariah names "the mourning of Megiddo" as the pattern for a greater mourning when Jerusalem looks on "him whom they have pierced" (Zech. 12:10–11). The connection is typological: a righteous, reforming king cut off prematurely, mourned by the whole nation, prefigures the death of the Righteous One whose death is mourned by all who come to see it clearly (Rev. 1:7). Unlike Josiah, whose reform could not avert exile, Christ's death accomplishes the definitive end of exile — "he has broken down the dividing wall of hostility" (Eph. 2:14), opening the new covenant that Josiah's reform only foreshadowed.
